does: This paper proposes APLOT, an enhancement to Bradley-Terry-based reward models for LLM alignment that addresses the challenge of distinguishing between similar preference pairs. The core contribution is an adaptive margin mechanism that dynamically adjusts focus on harder samples using optimal transport theory. The method constructs a distributional cost matrix incorporating semantic similarity and model-predicted reward differences, enabling the reward model to automatically scale margins based on sample difficulty. The approach maintains the simplicity of pairwise preference learning while improving robustness through principled margin allocation, with experiments demonstrating better generalization to out-of-distribution samples and reduced overfitting compared to standard Bradley-Terry objectives.
connects: This work builds on decades of preference learning research, particularly the Bradley-Terry model widely adopted for human-aligned reward modeling in recent RLHF pipelines. It responds to known limitations in BT-based approaches regarding margin collapse and poor generalization to distributional shift, problems identified in the reward modeling literature for LLM alignment. The paper integrates optimal transport theory, an increasingly popular tool in machine learning for comparing distributions and designing robust learning objectives, adapting it specifically for adaptive margin learning. It also connects to broader work on hard-sample mining and curriculum learning, which similarly seek to focus training on difficult examples, but applies these ideas through a principled distributional lens rather than simpler heuristic approaches.
enables: This adaptive margin framework opens several research directions in robust reward modeling. The optimal transport perspective on margin design could extend to other preference learning settings beyond pairwise comparisons, such as ranking from sets or relative feedback. The approach may enable more efficient human preference annotation by automatically identifying genuinely ambiguous cases versus easy distinctions, potentially reducing labeling burden. Future work could investigate how these robust reward models scale to larger preference datasets and whether OT-based margins transfer across different LLM scales. The framework also suggests applications to domain adaptation scenarios where reward models must generalize across distribution shifts, and could inspire similar adaptive mechanisms in other alignment techniques beyond reward modeling.
